How do I get my music into video games? I play a lot of games with great soundtracks and I think my music could fit. [Context: {"currentModule":"licensing","hasProject":true}]

Video game sync is one of the fastest-growing licensing markets and it's MORE accessible to indie artists than film/TV sync. Here's why and how:

Why games are great for indie music: (1) Games use MORE music than movies — a single game might license 50-200 songs. (2) Indie aesthetic is valued — many games specifically want underground music for authenticity. (3) Games have long shelf lives — a song in a game earns royalties for YEARS as the game continues selling. (4) Massive exposure — popular games have millions of players hearing your music.

Types of game music placements: (1) Licensed soundtrack: your existing recording is used in the game (like GTA, FIFA, Need for Speed, Tony Hawk). This requires both a master and sync license. (2) In-game radio: your song plays on a fictional radio station within the game world. (3) Trailers/marketing: your music is used in game trailers and ads (often highest-paying). (4) Rhythm games: Beat Saber, Fortnite Festival, etc. — these have dedicated music licensing teams.

How to approach game studios: (1) Identify upcoming games in genres that match your music. Follow game studios on social media, subscribe to industry outlets (GameSpot, IGN, Game Developer). (2) Find the music supervisor or audio director. AAA studios have in-house music supervisors. Indie game developers often handle music themselves. LinkedIn is the best tool for finding the right contact. (3) Prepare your pitch: a short curated playlist (not your entire catalog — 5-8 tracks). Stems/instrumentals available. A one-paragraph intro about your sound and aesthetic. Your music on streaming platforms for easy preview.

Submission platforms: Songtradr (has specific game licensing categories). Musicbed (curated, some game clients). Artlist (growing game client base). Direct submission to game studio websites (many have music submission forms).

Licensing fees: Indie games: $500-$5,000 per song. AAA games: $5,000-$50,000+ per song. Game trailers: $10,000-$100,000+. These are wide ranges — negotiation depends on the game's budget, your profile, and the scope of use.

What to have ready: (1) Instrumentals (mandatory — many games need music without vocals). (2) Stems (separated tracks — some games need interactive audio). (3) Clean versions (no explicit language for games targeting younger audiences). (4) 30-second, 60-second, and 90-second edits. (5) Clear rights documentation — confirm you own master AND publishing (or have authority to license both).

A coffee shop asked if they need to pay me to play my album in their store. How does music licensing work for businesses? [Context: {"currentModule":"licensing","hasProject":true}]

Great question — and the answer involves the blanket licensing system that generates billions in music royalties. Here's how it works:

The law: Any business that plays music publicly (shops, restaurants, bars, gyms, offices) needs a public performance license. Playing Spotify through speakers in a business is a public performance, even though the business has a Spotify subscription. Spotify's terms of service explicitly state that personal subscriptions are not licensed for commercial use.

How businesses legally play music: They purchase blanket licenses from the PROs: ASCAP: annual license for businesses (cost varies by business size, type, and square footage. A small coffee shop might pay $300-$800/year). BMI: similar pricing. SESAC: similar, but smaller catalog. A blanket license gives the business permission to play ANY song in that PRO's catalog. Some businesses also use commercial streaming services: Soundtrack Your Brand (Spotify's commercial offering). Cloud Cover Music. Rockbot. These services include the blanket license in their subscription fee.

How you get paid: When the coffee shop pays ASCAP $500/year for their blanket license, that money goes into ASCAP's general pool. ASCAP distributes royalties based on a combination of: streaming data, radio play data, survey data, and cue sheet data. For indie artists, the per-play royalty from a single coffee shop is microscopic — fractions of a cent. BUT these royalties compound across thousands of businesses.

Should the coffee shop pay YOU directly? No — the blanket license system means they pay the PRO, not individual artists. You earn your share through your PRO membership.

What YOU need to do: Ensure your songs are registered with your PRO (ASCAP or BMI). Use a PRO that actively monitors business performances (both ASCAP and BMI do this). Consider registering with both a PRO and SoundExchange for different types of public performance income.

If the business asks you for a DIRECT license (bypassing the PRO system): this is unusual for background music but can happen for featured use (a fitness studio wanting to build a class around your music). In that case, negotiate a direct sync-style license.

A music supervisor asked if any of my tracks use AI-generated elements. Why does this matter for sync? [Context: {"currentModule":"licensing","hasProject":true}]

This is a critical question in 2026 sync licensing. Here's why supervisors ask:

1. Clearance Risk: Sync deals require 100% clearable rights. If part of your track is AI-generated and not copyrightable (per current U.S. Copyright Office guidance), the licensee's exclusive rights to that portion may be unenforceable. A competitor could theoretically use those same AI-generated elements with no infringement claim.

2. Liability Concerns: Studios and networks are increasingly cautious. If an AI model was trained on copyrighted music (most were), there's ongoing litigation risk. Some studios have blanket policies against AI-generated content in their productions to avoid being named in future lawsuits.

3. Union and Guild Issues: SAG-AFTRA and WGA contracts now include AI disclosure requirements. Music used in union productions may face additional scrutiny.

What to do: (1) Be honest about AI usage — misrepresenting your music's provenance can void a sync license and invite legal action. (2) If you used AI tools for assistance (mixing suggestions, mastering, arrangement ideas) but performed and recorded everything yourself, that's generally fine — disclose it but it's human-authored. (3) If AI generated substantial musical elements (melody, beat, harmony), disclose this and understand some sync opportunities may be off the table. (4) Create fully human-performed versions of your best sync candidates.

The landscape is evolving. Artists who maintain clean, fully human-authored catalogs have a competitive advantage in sync placement right now.

A couple asked if they can use my song as their first dance at their wedding. Should I charge them? Is there even a market for this? [Context: {"currentModule":"licensing","hasProject":true}]

This is a sweet request and it has both a feel-good answer and a business answer:

The feel-good answer: For a personal, private wedding ceremony, there's typically no license required. Playing a song at a private event (like a wedding reception in a rented venue) falls under the venue's existing blanket license from ASCAP/BMI. You don't need to charge the couple for playing your song at their wedding.

The business opportunity: However, there IS a licensing question if: (1) The wedding videographer wants to include your music in the wedding video that will be posted on YouTube or social media. This IS a sync license situation. A wedding video with your music that goes viral is now a commercial use of your composition and recording. (2) The couple wants to use your music in their wedding invitation videos, save-the-dates, or social media content.

How to handle it graciously AND capitalize: For the wedding itself: grant free permission with a warm note. This builds incredible loyalty and word-of-mouth. For the wedding video: offer a simple personal-use license. Pricing: $50-$100 for non-commercial personal use (posted to personal social media only). $200-$500 for commercial use (videographer uses it as portfolio content, couple posts widely). Create a simple license template: 'This license grants [couple's names] permission to include the song [title] in their wedding video for personal/non-commercial use. This license does not transfer any ownership rights.'

The hidden market: Wedding and event licensing is a legitimate income stream: (1) List your music on wedding music platforms like The Knot Marketplace and WeddingWire. (2) Create a 'wedding and events' section on your website with licensable songs. (3) Offer custom performances: live ceremony performances ($300-$2,000), custom-written first dance songs ($500-$3,000). (4) Partner with wedding videographers who need licensable music for their work.

Content ID consideration: If the wedding video ends up on YouTube and your music is registered with Content ID, it will trigger a claim. Be prepared to whitelist wedding videos if you've granted permission — communicate this with your distributor.

The long play: Every wedding guest who hears your song and asks 'who sings this?' is a potential new fan. Consider wedding plays as marketing opportunities.

How do I get my music into video games? I make ambient electronic stuff that would be perfect for game soundtracks. [Context: {"currentModule":"licensing","hasProject":true}]

Video game music licensing is a massive and growing market — the global gaming industry generates more revenue than film and music combined. Ambient electronic is one of the most in-demand genres for games. Here's how to get in:

Types of game music opportunities:
(1) Licensed soundtrack: your existing recordings are placed in the game's world (playing on an in-game radio, during menus, in trailers). Games like Grand Theft Auto, FIFA/EA Sports FC, Tony Hawk, and Fortnite actively license indie music. Fee: $1,000-$50,000+ depending on the game's budget and scope.
(2) Original score commission: a game studio hires you to compose original music specifically for their game. This is the most lucrative path — contracts range from $5,000 to $300,000+ for AAA titles. Indie games: $500-$10,000 for a full soundtrack (often including revenue share).
(3) Music packs/asset libraries: create game-ready music assets sold through platforms like Unity Asset Store, the Unreal Engine Marketplace, or GameDev Market. These generate passive income ($5-$50 per sale, recurring).
(4) Interactive/adaptive music: composing music that responds to gameplay (layers added/removed based on player actions). This is a specialized skill that commands premium rates.

How to get placed:
(1) Direct outreach to indie game developers: platforms like itch.io, IndieDB, and the r/gamedev subreddit are where indie developers congregate. Many indie devs need music but can't afford dedicated composers. Offer: 'I'll score your game for [fee/revenue share].' Your ambient electronic style is perfect for indie puzzle games, exploration games, and atmospheric horror.
(2) Music libraries for games: Artlist, Epidemic Sound, and Musicbed all serve game developers. Submit your catalog. Pond5 and AudioJungle have dedicated game music sections.
(3) Game music communities: join Game Audio Network Guild (GANG). Participate in game jams (48-72 hour game development events) — this gets your music into games and builds relationships with developers. Attend GDC (Game Developers Conference) or PAX.
(4) Game audio directors: these are the 'music supervisors' of the gaming world. Build relationships via LinkedIn, industry events, and by demonstrating your understanding of interactive audio.

Making your music 'game-ready': Seamless loops: game music often needs to loop without audible transitions. Create loop points. Stems and layers: separate your tracks into individual layers (ambient pad, rhythmic element, melodic element) that can be triggered independently by the game engine. Multiple intensity levels: 'exploration' version (calm), 'combat' version (intense), 'menu' version (minimal). File format: WAV or FLAC (not MP3) — games need lossless audio.

A music supervisor for a Netflix show emailed asking for my rate card. I've never been asked this before. What do I charge? [Context: {"currentModule":"licensing","hasProject":true}]

Congratulations — this is a great opportunity. Don't undersell yourself but also don't price yourself out. Here's how to approach it:

First, understand what they're asking for: A rate card quotes your fees for both the Master Use license (you own the recording) and the Sync license (you own the composition). If you're fully independent and own both, quote both together.

Fee ranges for Netflix/streaming shows in 2026: Background use (10-30 seconds, under dialogue): $2,000-$8,000 all-in. Featured use (30-60 seconds, emotional scene): $5,000-$20,000 all-in. End credits: $3,000-$10,000. Main title/theme: $15,000-$50,000+.

How to structure your response: Quote a range, not a fixed number. Example: 'For a featured placement on a Netflix original, our all-in rate (master + sync) is $7,500-$15,000 depending on usage, duration, and territory. We're open to discussing terms that work for the production.' Always ask: What scene? How long is the usage? What territories? How long is the license term? Is it exclusive to this project?

Negotiation tips: (1) MFN (Most Favored Nations) — request that your fee matches whatever the highest-paid indie track in the same episode receives. (2) Don't accept buyouts (perpetual, worldwide, all media) at the first price — negotiate step deals. (3) Retain your streaming/download rights — only license the sync right. (4) Request a screen credit if possible.

If you feel overwhelmed, a sync agent or entertainment attorney can negotiate on your behalf for 10-15% commission. At this level, it's often worth it.

How do I actually get my music in TV shows and movies? I hear about sync licensing but I have no idea how to break in. [Context: {"currentModule":"licensing","hasProject":true}]

Sync licensing is when your music is synchronized to visual media — TV, film, ads, video games, trailers. It's one of the highest-paying revenue streams for indie artists. Here's how to break in:

Understanding the deal: Every sync requires TWO licenses — Master license (for the recording, controlled by whoever owns the master — usually you if you're indie) and Sync license (for the composition, controlled by the songwriter/publisher). As a fully independent artist who writes and records your own music, you control BOTH — this is a massive advantage because music supervisors can clear your music with one email.

How music gets placed: (1) Music supervisors browse sync libraries and receive pitches from publishers/agents. (2) Supervisors search by mood, genre, tempo, lyrical theme — NOT by artist name or fame. (3) They need music that is 100% clearable with no unresolved splits or samples.

How to get started: (1) Register with sync libraries: Musicbed (selective, higher-end), Songtradr (open, non-exclusive), Artlist (subscription model), Music Gateway, Taxi (paid membership). (2) Create instrumental versions of every track — supervisors often need vocals-free versions. (3) Tag your music meticulously: mood, genre, tempo, instrumentation, lyrical themes. (4) Ensure your splits are documented and all contributors have signed off.

What pays: TV background placement (10-60 seconds): $500-$5,000. Featured TV placement (with vocals, emotional scene): $5,000-$25,000. Major ad campaign: $10,000-$100,000+. Indie film: $500-$3,000. Video game: $1,000-$25,000.

Pro tip: Join the Guild of Music Supervisors mailing list. Attend sync conferences (SXSW, A2IM Indie Week, Sync Summit). Build relationships — sync is a relationship business.

I'm looking at a sub-publishing deal for Europe. They're offering 75/25 on receipts. My US admin publisher says I should push for at-source. What's the difference and which is better? [Context: {"artistProfile":{"goal":"fully-independent","stage":"established","genre":"electronic"}}]

Your US publisher is right — always push for at-source. Here's why the difference is significant:

**At-source:** The sub-publisher's commission is calculated at the source — where the money originates. If your song earns €10,000 in Germany, and the sub-publisher takes 25%, you get €7,500. Your US admin publisher then takes their cut from that €7,500. One deduction at the origin point.

**On-receipts:** The sub-publisher takes their 25% (€2,500). Then they send €7,500 to your US admin publisher. Your US admin takes THEIR commission (say 15%) from what they receive — that's another €1,125 off. You end up with €6,375 instead of €7,500. On €10,000 earned, you lost an extra €1,125 to the double-dip.

**The math at scale:**
If your European publishing generates €100,000/year:
- At-source (25% sub-pub, 15% admin): You keep €63,750
- On-receipts (same percentages): You keep €63,750... wait, same? No — the compounding changes. At-source: €100k × 0.75 = €75k → admin takes 15% of €75k = €11,250. You keep €63,750. On-receipts: €100k × 0.75 = €75k sent to admin → admin takes 15% of €75k = same calculation IF admin only charges on what they receive. The real issue: some admin deals calculate on gross catalog revenue, meaning that €100k shows as your gross and they take 15% of €100k = €15,000, PLUS the sub-pub already took €25,000. Now you're at €60,000.

**Non-negotiable terms to demand:**
1. At-source accounting — always
2. Sub-publisher commission capped at 20-25% maximum
3. Minimum performance commitment (they must actively pitch your catalog in-territory)
4. Reversion clause: if revenue drops below a threshold for 2 consecutive years, rights revert
5. Audit rights in-territory (you can audit the sub-publisher directly, not just through your admin)

I'm making a documentary for a nonprofit and want to use 3 popular songs in it. Since it's for charity, I don't need to license them, right? [Context: {"currentModule":"licensing","hasProject":true}]

This is a very common misconception — nonprofit status does NOT exempt you from music licensing requirements. You absolutely still need to clear the rights.

The myth: 'If it's not for profit, it's fair use.' This is incorrect. The 'purpose and character of the use' is only ONE of four fair use factors, and nonprofit use does not automatically qualify as fair use. The other factors (nature of the copyrighted work, amount used, and market impact) all weigh against using popular songs in a documentary.

What you need: For each of the 3 songs, you need: (1) A sync license from the publisher (for the composition). (2) A master use license from the label/master owner (for the recording).

Nonprofit licensing options: (1) Negotiate directly — explain the nonprofit nature and ask for a reduced fee or gratis (free) license. Some publishers and labels grant reduced-fee licenses for legitimate nonprofit projects, especially if the organization's mission aligns with the rights holder's values. (2) Many popular songs are unaffordable even at reduced rates. Consider: using indie/unsigned music instead (much easier to clear and much cheaper), commissioning original music, using music from royalty-free libraries (Epidemic Sound, Artlist), or finding songs under Creative Commons licenses.

An alternative: approach artists directly through social media. Many indie artists will grant a free sync license for a nonprofit documentary in exchange for credit and exposure. Major artists' teams may also consider gratis licenses but require formal requests through their publishers.

Risk of using music without permission: The rights holders can: demand the documentary be pulled from all platforms, sue for statutory damages ($750-$150,000 per infringement), and the nonprofit's 501(c)(3) status does not shield against copyright infringement lawsuits.

Bottom line: always license. But negotiate aggressively on the nonprofit angle — many rights holders will accommodate generous terms for genuinely charitable projects.

Songtradr wants me to submit my music non-exclusively, but another library wants an exclusive deal for 3 years. What's the difference and which is better? [Context: {"currentModule":"licensing","hasProject":true}]

This is one of the most important decisions in sync licensing strategy. Here's the breakdown:

Non-Exclusive: You license the same track to multiple libraries simultaneously. Each library can pitch it, but none owns it. You can submit to Songtradr, Musicbed, Music Gateway, and more all at once. Pros: Maximum exposure, you retain full control, can pull music anytime. Cons: Less incentive for any single library to push your music hard. Some libraries 'retitle' your tracks (give them a different name for licensing) — this can create metadata conflicts with your PRO.

Exclusive: One library controls all sync licensing for that specific track (or your entire catalog) for a set term. Pros: The library is financially incentivized to place your music (they have guaranteed exclusivity). Often comes with better pitching, higher placement rates, and sometimes an advance. Cons: You can't license those tracks anywhere else during the term. If the library underperforms, your music sits idle for 3 years.

My recommendation: For most indie artists in 2026, start non-exclusive across 3-5 libraries (Songtradr, Musicbed, Music Gateway). Track which libraries actually generate placements over 6-12 months. If one library consistently performs, consider giving them exclusive rights to your NEXT batch of tracks — not your existing catalog.

Red flags in exclusive deals: Terms longer than 3 years. Automatic renewal clauses. Rights to future works you haven't created yet. No minimum placement guarantees or marketing commitments. Contracts that extend to your streaming/distribution rights (they should only control sync).

Always have an entertainment attorney review an exclusive sync deal before signing.

I found a Creative Commons track I want to sample. What are my rights? [Context: {"currentModule":"agent","hasProject":true}]

Creative Commons has 6 license types — they're NOT all the same:

- **CC BY** — Use freely, just credit the creator
- **CC BY-SA** — Use freely, credit, and share your work under the same license
- **CC BY-NC** — Attribution required, NO commercial use
- **CC BY-ND** — Attribution required, no modifications (can't sample)
- **CC0** — Public domain, no restrictions

I need to check which specific CC license applies. Running `check_availability` with the source URL. The 'NC' and 'ND' variants are the ones that cause problems for commercial releases.

A brand wants to use my song in a TikTok ad campaign. Is that different from someone using it in a regular TikTok video? [Context: {"artistProfile":{"goal":"fully-independent","stage":"developing","genre":"r&b"}}]

Completely different — and this is a distinction that costs artists real money when they don't understand it.

**Regular TikTok videos (UGC — User Generated Content):** TikTok has blanket licensing agreements with distributors and labels. When a random user puts your song on their TikTok, you get a small royalty through your distributor. You already opted into this when you distributed your music. No additional licensing needed.

**Brand using your song in a TikTok AD:** This is commercial advertising. The blanket license does NOT cover this. A brand running paid campaigns using your music needs a separate sync license from you — for both the master and publishing. This is a negotiable deal with real money attached.

**What to charge:** Depends on the brand size, campaign duration, and territory. Rough ranges:
- Small/local brand, 3-month campaign, US-only: $2,000-$10,000
- Mid-size brand, 6-month campaign, North America: $10,000-$50,000
- Major brand, 12-month global: $50,000-$250,000+

**Advocate position:** If a brand reaches out to 'use your song on TikTok,' your first question should be: 'Is this for paid advertising or organic content?' If they say ads, this is a sync deal. Don't let them imply it's covered by TikTok's platform license — it's not. Get the scope in writing (duration, territory, exclusivity, platforms) before quoting a price. And don't undersell — they came to you because your song fits their campaign. That's worth real money.

How do I get my music in video games? I know big games use licensed music but is there an opportunity for indie artists? [Context: {"currentModule":"licensing","hasProject":true}]

Video game sync is one of the fastest-growing licensing markets and indie artists have real advantages here. Here's the landscape:

Types of game music opportunities: AAA titles (Call of Duty, FIFA/EA Sports FC, GTA): Pay $5,000-$100,000+ per track. Highly competitive. Usually sourced through music supervisors and publishers. Indie/mid-tier games: Pay $500-$5,000 per track. MUCH more accessible. Indie game developers actively seek out indie musicians. Mobile games: Pay $200-$2,000 per track. High volume, lower rates but can add up. Trailers/marketing: Often pay MORE than in-game placement ($2,000-$25,000).

Why indie artists have an advantage: (1) Indie game developers prefer working with indie musicians — shared ethos, flexible on terms, easier to clear rights. (2) You own your masters AND publishing, meaning the developer only needs one clearance conversation. Major-label artists require multiple sign-offs. (3) Niche genres (lo-fi, ambient, electronic, chiptune, synthwave) are in high demand for games.

How to get placements: (1) Network in indie game communities: Reddit (r/gamedev, r/IndieDev), Discord servers for game jams, X/Twitter gamedev community. Many developers post music-needed calls. (2) Attend game industry events: GDC (Game Developers Conference), PAX, IndieCade, game jams. (3) Register with game-focused sync libraries: APM Music, Marmoset Music, Artlist (games category). (4) Create a game-ready portfolio: upload instrumental tracks on Bandcamp with 'available for licensing' in the description. Include: genre, BPM, mood, loopable versions.

Licensing structure for games: Typically a flat buyout fee (one-time payment, no ongoing royalties). Some deals include backend royalties if the game exceeds certain sales thresholds. Negotiate: non-exclusive (you can still use the music elsewhere) vs exclusive (only in that game). Territory and term — avoid perpetual worldwide if possible.

Pro tip: Offer 'loopable' versions of your tracks (seamless loops for background music). This makes you much more attractive to game developers.

A sync agency wants to represent my catalog exclusively for 3 years. They take 50% of any placements they land. The alternative is staying non-exclusive on Songtradr where I keep 65%. Which is better? [Context: {"artistProfile":{"goal":"fully-independent","stage":"established","genre":"electronic"}}]

This is one of the most consequential decisions in sync licensing. Let me analyze the contract terms and model both scenarios.

**Option A — Exclusive Sync Agency (50% commission, 3-year term):**

✅ **What you get:**
- Active pitching by a dedicated team with relationships (this is HUGE — supervisors trust their known agents)
- They do the outreach, negotiation, and paperwork
- Potentially higher-value placements through established connections
- Some agencies provide upfront catalog evaluation and tagging

❌ **What you give up:**
- 50% of every placement fee (industry standard is 30-40% — 50% is high)
- EXCLUSIVE means you cannot pitch your own music for sync during the term
- EXCLUSIVE means you cannot use Songtradr, Musicbed, or any other platform
- If they don't actively pitch your catalog, your music sits dormant for 3 years
- You can't license your own music if someone contacts you directly

**Option B — Non-Exclusive on Songtradr (35% commission):**

✅ **What you get:**
- You keep your music available everywhere simultaneously
- If someone contacts you directly, you keep 100%
- Freedom to add/remove songs anytime
- No lock-in period

❌ **What you give up:**
- No active pitching — you're waiting to be discovered
- Lower placement rates (Songtradr is marketplace pricing, not boutique)
- No personal relationship with supervisors on your behalf

**The math over 3 years (hypothetical):**

| Scenario | Placements | Avg Fee | Gross | Your Take |
|----------|-----------|---------|-------|----------|
| Exclusive agency (active pitching) | 8 placements | $5,000 | $40,000 | $20,000 (50%) |
| Songtradr (passive) | 3 placements | $2,000 | $6,000 | $3,900 (65%) |
| Hybrid (non-exclusive agency at 35%) | 5 placements | $3,500 | $17,500 | $11,375 (65%) |

**Advocate position:** The exclusive deal MIGHT generate more total revenue — but only if the agency is genuinely active. Ask them:
1. How many placements have they made in the last 12 months for similar catalogs?
2. Can they name specific supervisors they pitch to regularly?
3. Will they provide quarterly activity reports?
4. **CRITICAL: Negotiate the commission to 35-40%.** 50% is above market.
5. **Add a performance clause:** If they land fewer than 3 placements in the first year, you can terminate.
6. **Reduce term to 18 months** with option to renew.

Never sign an exclusive deal without a performance clause. An exclusive with no activity = your catalog in a drawer.

A video game studio wants to put my song in their indie game. How are game sync deals different from film/TV, and what should I negotiate? [Context: {"artistProfile":{"goal":"fully-independent","stage":"developing","genre":"electronic"}}]

Game sync is structurally different from film/TV — and the differences generally favor the licensee more than the artist if you're not careful. Here's what you need to know:

**Key differences from film/TV sync:**

1. **Term:** Games almost always ask for perpetual, worldwide rights. Unlike a TV placement that might be term-limited, games live forever on platforms. Once your song is in a game, it stays there as long as the game is sold.

2. **No backend royalties (usually):** Film/TV sync generates performance royalties every time the show airs (your PRO pays you). Games generate NO performance royalties because gameplay is classified as interactive, not broadcast. Your sync fee is typically your only payment.

3. **Buyout structure:** Because there are no backend royalties, the upfront fee should be HIGHER than a comparable TV placement. Many game studios try to pay film/TV rates without acknowledging the no-backend difference. Push back.

**Pricing for indie games:**
- Small indie game (team of <10, limited distribution): $1,000-5,000
- Mid-size indie (publisher-backed, console release): $5,000-15,000
- AAA game: $15,000-100,000+ (usually handled through agencies)

**What to negotiate:**
1. **Higher upfront fee** to compensate for zero performance royalties
2. **Credit:** Your artist name and song title visible in-game and in credits. This drives streaming discovery — players Shazam songs in games constantly.
3. **Non-exclusive:** You can still license the song to other games, films, TV, etc. unless they pay a significant premium for exclusivity.
4. **Promotional rights:** The right to create content showing your music in the game (for your social media). Some studios restrict this — push back.
5. **Sequel/DLC rights:** If the deal covers only the base game, any expansion packs, sequels, or DLC require a new negotiation. Don't let them bundle 'all future products' into the original fee.

**For electronic music specifically:** Game sync is one of the highest-impact placements. Electronic music fits gaming culture naturally, discovery is organic (players hear your track repeatedly over hours of gameplay), and AAA game soundtracks generate Spotify playlists with millions of followers.

I make high-energy electronic music. How can I get it used in fitness classes, workout playlists, and gym apps? [Context: {"currentModule":"licensing","hasProject":true}]

Fitness is one of the biggest and fastest-growing sync markets — and it's tailor-made for high-energy electronic music. Here are the paths:

Major fitness platforms: Peloton: uses licensed music extensively in their classes. They work with music supervisors who curate for specific workout types. Getting in: submit through Peloton's music submission portal (if available) or through sync libraries that work with fitness clients (Musicbed, Songtradr). Pay: per-use license, typically $500-$5,000 depending on usage scope. Apple Fitness+: curated playlists for workout classes. Submit through music libraries or through Apple Music editorial contacts. Equinox/SoulCycle/Barry's: Individual studios often use blanket-licensed music through services like Soundtrack Your Brand, but sometimes license specific tracks for branded content.

Fitness apps: Tempo, FitOn, Alo Moves, Nike Training Club — many license music for their instructional content. Submit through sync libraries or directly approach their content teams on LinkedIn.

Aerobic music companies: Yes! Fitness Music (yesfitness.com): specifically creates and licenses music for group fitness classes. They commission artists to create music at specific BPMs (128-140 BPM for aerobics, 140-160 for HIIT). Power Music: similar model, licensing music for fitness instructors.

Direct-to-instructor: Create BPM-locked mixes or tracks and sell them directly to fitness instructors on platforms like: Fitness Music Shop, Traxsource (DJ/fitness market), your own website.

What fitness supervisors look for: High energy, consistent tempo. Clean/radio-safe (no explicit lyrics). Predictable structure (builds and drops that match workout intervals — this is CRITICAL). Specific BPM ranges: yoga/cool down: 60-80 BPM, cycling: 120-140 BPM, HIIT: 140-170 BPM, running: 150-180 BPM. Motivational or neutral lyrics. Professional mix quality — fitness classes use full-range speakers.

Positioning your catalog: Tag all your tracks with BPM and energy level. Create instrumental versions (many classes prefer no vocals). Create workout-friendly editsmatching typical class segments (30-second intros, 32-beat phrases, clean endings). Submit to fitness-focused playlist curators on Spotify.

The scale opportunity: One placement in a Peloton class can expose your music to 100,000+ listeners in a week. The fitness licensing market is growing at 15-20% annually. It's less competitive than traditional sync because fewer artists specifically target this market.

A popular podcast wants to use my song as their intro music. What should I charge and how does the licensing work? [Context: {"currentModule":"licensing","hasProject":true}]

Podcast licensing is a growing market in 2026. Podcasts need music for intros, outros, transitions, and bed music — and many are willing to pay for quality indie music. Here's how to approach it:

Rate card for podcast music licensing: Small podcast (under 10k downloads/episode): $200-$500 for a 1-year license. Mid-tier podcast (10k-100k downloads): $500-$2,000 for a 1-year license. Major podcast (100k+ downloads, network-backed): $2,000-$10,000+ per year. Celebrity/branded podcast: $5,000-$25,000+ (often negotiated by the network's music supervisor).

Key terms to negotiate: (1) Duration — how long can they use it? Standard: 1 year with renewal option. Don't give perpetual rights. (2) Usage — intro only? Intro + outro? All transitions and bed music? Price scales with usage breadth. (3) Exclusivity — can you license the same track to other podcasts? Non-exclusive is standard; if they want exclusive, charge 2-3x. (4) Territory — US only or worldwide? (5) Derivative works — can they remix, edit, or shorten your track? (6) Credit — require an audio credit ('Music by [Your Name]') and a link in show notes. This is valuable marketing.

How to structure your quote: 'For use as intro/outro music on [Podcast Name] (non-exclusive, worldwide, 1-year term): $750 all-in (master + sync). Renewal for subsequent years: $500/year. Audio credit and show notes link required.'

Finding podcast licensing opportunities: Register on Musicbed, Artlist, and Epidemic Sound (all serve podcast buyers). Market directly to podcasters in your genre niche. Create 'podcast-ready' versions of your music: 15-30 second edits with clean intros and outros.

Pro tip: Podcast intro music plays on EVERY episode. A weekly podcast with 50k downloads uses your 15 seconds of music 52 times per year, exposing your name to 2.6 million listens. Factor in the marketing value when pricing — sometimes a lower fee with guaranteed credit is worth more than a higher fee with no attribution.

I've heard that getting a song in a commercial can pay really well. How does advertising sync licensing work and how do I position myself? [Context: {"currentModule":"licensing","hasProject":true}]

Advertising sync is the highest-paying sync category — and it's more accessible to indie artists than you might think. Here's the landscape:

Why advertising pays more: Commercials have massive budgets and short timelines. A 30-second TV spot for a national brand can have a $50M ad spend — the music license is a tiny fraction. Music is CRITICAL to commercial effectiveness (studies show music increases ad recall by 96%).

Fee ranges: Local/regional ads: $1,000-$10,000. National TV commercial: $25,000-$300,000. Global campaign (multi-territory): $100,000-$1,000,000+. Social media/digital-only ads: $2,000-$25,000. Super Bowl commercials: $200,000-$2,000,000+ (outlier but aspirational).

Licensing structure for advertising: (1) Sync fee — the upfront payment for using your song. Negotiated based on: media spend (local vs national vs global), duration (13 weeks, 26 weeks, 1 year), territory (US only, North America, worldwide), and media type (TV, digital, cinema, all media). (2) Performance royalties — your PRO (ASCAP/BMI) collects performance royalties every time the commercial airs. A national TV ad can generate $10,000-$50,000+ in performance royalties over its run. This is SEPARATE from and IN ADDITION TO the sync fee.

How to get placed: (1) Music supervisors at ad agencies find music through: sync agents/representatives (best path), music libraries (Musicbed, Songtradr, Songfinch), direct artist websites and Spotify playlists, personal recommendation from other supervisors. (2) Position your catalog: tag your music by mood, energy, and use case ('feel-good,' 'inspiring,' 'dramatic'). Create instrumentals for every track (ads often use instrumentals). Have stems available (drums, bass, vocals separately). (3) Build relationships: attend advertising industry events (AdWeek, Cannes Lions adjacent events). Follow and engage with music supervisors on LinkedIn and Instagram. Join the Guild of Music Supervisors database.

What makes music 'sync-ready' for advertising: Positive, uplifting energy (65%+ of commercial syncs are feel-good). Strong, memorable hooks (the melody needs to stick in 15-30 seconds). Clean recordings (professional mix and master). Available in multiple formats (full, instrumental, stems, edits). And most importantly: NO uncleared samples.

The indie advantage: As an independent artist who owns both master and publishing, you can offer a 'one-stop' license. The agency clears everything with ONE email and ONE payment. For major label artists, they need to negotiate with the label AND the publisher separately — this can take weeks. Your one-stop advantage makes you faster, simpler, and more attractive for tight timelines.

A fitness brand wants to use my song in their Instagram ads. Is this a sync deal? How much should I charge? [Context: {"currentModule":"licensing","hasProject":true}]

Yes, this IS technically a sync license (music synchronized to visual media), but brand/advertising sync has its own rate structure and terms that differ from TV/film sync.

Rate card for social media ad licensing in 2026: One platform (Instagram only), 3-month term: $1,000-$5,000. Multi-platform (Instagram + TikTok + YouTube), 6-month term: $3,000-$15,000. National broadcast (TV commercial): $15,000-$100,000+. Global, all-media, 1-year: $10,000-$50,000+.

For a fitness brand using your song on Instagram ads specifically, a reasonable range is $1,500-$5,000 depending on: the brand's size (local gym vs Peloton), the ad spend behind it (how many impressions), the duration of the license, and whether they want exclusivity in the fitness category.

Key terms to negotiate: (1) Term length — how long can they use it? 3 months, 6 months, 1 year? Don't give perpetual rights. (2) Territory — US only? Worldwide? (3) Platform — Instagram only? Or all social, web, and streaming platforms? (4) Exclusivity — can you license the same song to another fitness brand? If they want exclusivity, charge 2-3x. (5) Paid media vs organic — if they're putting ad spend behind it (paid media), the price goes UP significantly vs just posting organically. (6) Modifications — can they edit, remix, or shorten your track?

Structure your quote: 'For a 6-month, Instagram-only, non-exclusive license with paid media rights for [Brand Name]: $3,000 all-in (master + sync). Exclusivity within the fitness vertical for the license term: additional $1,500. Total: $4,500.'

Always get the deal in writing before delivering stems or high-quality files. And never send unwatermarked audio until payment or a signed agreement is received.

Someone wants to license my song for their indie film. I have no idea what to charge. What's fair? [Context: {"artistProfile":{"goal":"fully-independent","stage":"emerging","genre":"indie"}}]

The answer depends entirely on the film's budget, distribution, and what rights they're asking for. Here's how to figure out a fair price:

**Step 1 — Ask the right questions first:**
- What's the film's production budget? (student film = $0-500, indie feature with distribution = $2,000-25,000, studio-backed = $25,000+)
- How will the film be distributed? (Film festival only, streaming platform, theatrical release?)
- What kind of usage? (Background music in a scene, featured prominent use, opening/closing credits?)
- What territory and term? (US-only for 2 years vs. worldwide in perpetuity?)

**Step 2 — The pricing framework:**

| Film Budget | Background Use | Featured Use | Credits |
|------------|---------------|-------------|--------|
| Student/No budget | $0-250 (or credit only) | $250-500 | $500-1,000 |
| Indie ($50k-500k budget) | $500-2,000 | $2,000-5,000 | $3,000-7,500 |
| Indie w/ distribution deal | $2,500-5,000 | $5,000-15,000 | $10,000-25,000 |
| Studio/Major | $10,000-50,000 | $25,000-100,000+ | $50,000-250,000+ |

**Step 3 — What to include in the license:**
- You're licensing TWO rights: master (your recording) + publishing (your song composition). As an indie who owns both, you can quote a single fee covering both — this is a competitive advantage.
- Specify: territory (US vs worldwide), term (2 years vs perpetuity), media type (theatrical vs all media), exclusivity (non-exclusive is standard for indie sync)

**Advocate position:** Don't license for free unless it's a genuine student project and the exposure is real (festival circuit, notable director). Even small indie films have music budgets. Quoting $0 devalues every indie artist who negotiates after you. Start with a reasonable ask based on the table above and be willing to negotiate down 20-30% — but never start at zero.

I sampled from a cover version of a classic song, not the original. Is it different? [Context: {"currentModule":"agent","hasProject":true}]

Sampling a cover version requires TWO separate clearances:

1. **Master Use License** — from whoever owns the cover recording (the cover artist/label)
2. **Composition License** — from whoever owns the underlying song (the original songwriter/publisher)

Both are required. The cover recording is a separate master, but the composition rights belong to the original writers. I can run clearance checks for both.

A production company reached out about using one of my songs in a documentary. How do I check if the song is actually available for licensing and what I should charge? [Context: {"artistProfile":{"goal":"fully-independent","stage":"developing","genre":"folk"}}]

Great opportunity — documentaries are one of the best sync licensing channels for indie folk artists because the budgets are real and the audience is attentive. Let me check the licensing availability with `check_availability`.

**Availability Check Results:**
To license a song for sync, two things must be clear:
1. **Master recording:** You own it (or your label does). If you recorded it independently, you own it. ✅
2. **Composition:** You wrote it (or the splits are documented). If there are co-writers, ALL of them must agree to the sync license.

**If you own both (master + composition):**
This is your biggest competitive advantage as an indie artist. You're a one-stop shop — the production company only needs YOUR approval. Major label songs require clearing through two separate entities (label for master, publisher for composition), which takes weeks. You can clear in 24 hours.

**What to charge (documentary sync fees):**

| Documentary Type | Typical Fee Range | Notes |
|-----------------|-------------------|-------|
| Student/micro-budget | $500-1,500 | Worth it for the credit + exposure |
| Independent (festival circuit) | $1,500-5,000 | Standard for indie docs |
| Broadcast (Netflix, HBO, PBS) | $5,000-25,000 | Push for the higher end |
| Major theatrical release | $15,000-50,000+ | Get an entertainment attorney involved |

**Key terms to negotiate:**
- **Territory:** Worldwide vs. specific regions (worldwide = higher fee)
- **Term:** Perpetuity vs. 5-10 years (perpetuity = higher fee)
- **Media:** All media vs. specific platforms (broader = higher fee)
- **Exclusivity:** NEVER grant exclusivity unless the fee justifies losing other placements

**Advocate position:** Don't underprice yourself out of excitement. Ask the production company their budget first — they often have more than you'd expect. And ALWAYS get the fee in writing before delivering stems.

I keep getting DMs from YouTubers and podcasters asking to use my music. Should I let them? Should I charge? How does this work? [Context: {"currentModule":"licensing","hasProject":true}]

This is actually a growing market that many indie artists monetize effectively. Content creator licensing sits between 'free exposure' and 'full sync licensing.' Here's how to handle it:

The hierarchy of content creator requests: (1) Small creators (<10k subscribers): likely can't afford a license fee. Consider: granting free use with mandatory credit (link to your Spotify/social in their description). Why: genuine exposure to their audience, goodwill, and your music reaching new ears. (2) Mid-tier creators (10k-100k): can afford $25-$100 per song per video. Create a simple licensing form on your website or use a platform like SongFinch or Musicbed. (3) Large creators (100k-1M+): charge $100-$500+ per song. Negotiate based on: how many views they average, how prominently your music is featured, and whether it's a one-time use or blanket license. (4) Enterprise creators/companies (1M+ or brand accounts): $500-$5,000+ per song. These are real sync licenses.

The micro-licensing structure: A simple agreement should cover: what song(s) are being licensed, what platform(s) the content will appear on, whether the license is exclusive or non-exclusive, the duration (perpetual or time-limited), the fee (or 'gratis with credit'), and what attribution is required.

Platforms that automate this: Epidemic Sound, Artlist, Musicbed — join as an artist and let the platform handle licensing. They take 30-50% but eliminate the administrative headache. Lickd — specifically for YouTube creators licensing real music. Content ID considerations: if your music is registered with Content ID (via your distributor), a creator using your music without a license will trigger a claim. You can: whitelist specific videos/channels manually through your distributor, or issue Content ID waivers to creators who've licensed your music.

The 'free use' debate: Giving free licenses isn't always bad IF: the creator links to your Spotify/Apple Music. The creator has an audience that matches your target demographic. You get the content for YOUR social media too (cross-promotion). Don't give free licenses if: the creator won't credit you, your music is the primary draw of the content (they need YOUR sound specifically — that has value), or it's a commercial/brand-sponsored video (they have budget).

Template response for DMs: 'Hey! Thanks for reaching out — I appreciate you wanting to use my music! For creators under 10k followers, I'm happy to grant free use as long as you credit me with a link to [your Spotify]. For larger channels, I offer affordable micro-licenses starting at $50. Let me know your channel size and I can get you set up!'

What exactly is a sync license and how does it work? [Context: {"currentModule":"agent","hasProject":true}]

A **sync license** (synchronization license) grants permission to use a musical composition in visual media — film, TV, ads, video games, YouTube videos.

**How it works:**
1. A music supervisor identifies your track for placement
2. They negotiate a sync fee (ranges: $500 indie to $500K+ major)
3. You typically need TWO licenses: sync (composition) + master use (recording)
4. Upfront fee + potential backend royalties from broadcast

Sync is one of the most lucrative revenue streams for independent artists. Want me to research sync library submissions for your catalog?
